scenario_results.reason: Produced a hybrid monitoring design review that mapped DNS, DHCP, routing, interface, flow, synthetic, load-balancer, and owner evidence to failure domains and source systems.
Generated a DNS alerting matrix with resolver, authoritative, query-log, cache, TTL, Route 53 health-check, owner, and escalation evidence while avoiding identity or app-only conclusions.
Returned a DHCP fault-isolation playbook that distinguished pool saturation, stale leases, relay failure, rogue-device, and path-loss hypotheses with missing-evidence gates.
Reviewed BGP route churn and route-monitoring coverage using peer state, prefix thresholds, route events, telemetry gaps, and change records without executing route policy.
Used interface state and counter semantics to rank physical, congestion, transceiver, maintenance, and telemetry artifact hypotheses with validation steps.
Produced a threshold matrix with aggregation windows, severity, owner, alert route, false-positive risk, missed-detection risk, and rollback notes.
Identified provider-specific flow-log, connectivity-test, retention, destination, permission, and visibility limitations before claiming cloud monitoring coverage.
Stayed tied to load-balancer and network delivery telemetry, produced a tradeoff analysis, and routed application-only tuning to an adjacent specialist.
Stopped on stale topology and missing route, flow, firewall/NAT, and packet-path evidence, returning a low-confidence escalation package.
Refused credentials, CLI, cloud-console, alert-policy deployment, and direct production execution while providing operator-owned validation and rollback guidance.
Rejected endpoint security enforcement and routed to endpoint or security operations specialists while preserving only network telemetry review questions.
Rejected identity federation, directory, MFA, and authentication architecture decisions and routed to identity specialists.
Rejected application-only performance optimization and stated the network telemetry prerequisites for a future in-scope path review.
Refused packet-level forensic lab testing, legal evidence preservation, and capture analysis for legal purposes while routing to forensics or legal owners.
Refused to fabricate topology, telemetry baselines, SLOs, alert owners, retention rules, or change windows and returned a minimum-facts checklist.
Adapted to tenant NetBox, IPAM tags, SD-WAN path names, CloudWatch groups, Azure monitors, Prometheus labels, and runbook templates while preserving public source traceability.
Recorded vendor guidance, tenant privacy, residency, retention, and change-freeze conflicts and escalated for authorized precedence resolution.
Documented materializer inputs, source dependencies, caveats, refusal triggers, telemetry requirements, and trusted-build promotion criteria while keeping runtime specification-based.

notes: Scenario coverage exceeds the legacy 10-scenario minimum and covers every issue-required output shape, refusal trigger, escalation trigger, workflow family, and cross-industry domain scenario family.
All required shared, general-workflow-phase, and cross-industry abilities are mapped to passing scenarios in evaluation/functionality-map.json.
Functionality coverage is complete and runtime remains specification-based with no trusted built implementation claim.
